questions.question_text: A Florida brokerage opens a second location. What must the broker do regarding this branch office?
questions.correct_answer: Register it separately with DBPR
distractors: Obtain FREC approval at the next meeting
Simply update the main office records
No action is required for branches
questions.explanation: Every branch office must be separately registered with DBPR. Each branch must also have the required entrance sign showing the broker's name, trade name if any, and the words 'Licensed Real Estate Broker.'

questions.question_text: What must be displayed at the entrance of every Florida brokerage office?
questions.correct_answer: A sign with the broker's name and the words 'Licensed Real Estate Broker'
distractors: A copy of the broker's license photo
The names of all sales associates
questions.explanation: Section 475.22 requires an easily observed entrance sign at the principal office and every branch office. It must contain the broker's name (for a partnership or corporation, the firm name plus at least one broker's name), the trade name if one is used, and the words 'Licensed Real Estate Broker' or 'Lic. Real Estate Broker.'

questions.question_text: Every Florida brokerage must have at least how many offices registered with DBPR?
questions.correct_answer: One office
questions.explanation: Every brokerage must have at least one office registered with DBPR. Additional branch offices must also be separately registered. A virtual-only brokerage is not permitted.

questions.question_text: Is a Florida broker required to display their broker license inside the registered office?
questions.correct_answer: No — Florida requires an entrance sign, not license display
distractors: Yes, framed at the main entrance of each office
Yes, but only at branch offices
Yes, along with the licenses of all sales associates
questions.explanation: Florida law contains no requirement to display the broker's license in the office. What s. 475.22 requires is an office of at least one enclosed room in a building of stationary construction and an entrance sign at the principal office and each branch showing the broker's name, trade name if any, and 'Licensed Real Estate Broker.'

questions.question_text: A broker operates under a DBA name that is not registered with DBPR. This is considered:
questions.correct_answer: A violation of Florida license law
distractors: Legal as long as clients are told
questions.explanation: Brokerages may operate under a trade name (DBA) only if it is registered with DBPR. Using an unregistered trade name violates license law and can result in disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: Under Florida law, real estate commission rates are determined by:
questions.correct_answer: Negotiation between broker and client
distractors: State statute with a maximum cap
questions.explanation: Commission rates are always negotiable in Florida. There is no set or maximum rate. Any agreement among brokers to fix commission rates would be a violation of federal anti-trust laws.

questions.question_text: A buyer wants to pay a bonus directly to the sales associate who found their home. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, compensation must come from the employing broker
distractors: Yes, if the buyer puts the agreement in writing
Yes, if the total amount is under $1,000
Yes, if the employing broker expressly approves
questions.explanation: A Florida sales associate can ONLY receive compensation from their employing broker — never directly from a buyer, seller, or other broker. All compensation must flow through the employing broker.

questions.question_text: A sales associate earned a commission before transferring to a new broker. Who owes the commission?
questions.correct_answer: The previous employing broker
distractors: The client who paid the fee
questions.explanation: Commissions earned while registered under a previous broker must be paid by that previous broker. The new broker has no obligation to pay commissions earned under another brokerage.

questions.question_text: Three competing brokerages agree to charge the same commission rate. This practice violates which law?
questions.correct_answer: Federal anti-trust law
questions.explanation: Price-fixing of commission rates among brokers is a violation of federal anti-trust law (Sherman Act). Each brokerage must independently set its own commission rates through negotiation with clients.

questions.question_text: A sales associate receives a referral fee directly from an out-of-state broker. Under Florida law, this is:
questions.correct_answer: A violation — must go through the employing broker
distractors: Permitted for any interstate referral arrangement
Allowed if the total fee is under $500
Legal as long as there is written disclosure
questions.explanation: All compensation to a sales associate must come through their employing broker, including referral fees from out-of-state brokers. The out-of-state broker should pay the Florida employing broker, who then pays the associate.

questions.question_text: All Florida real estate advertising must include which identifying information?
questions.correct_answer: The registered name of the brokerage
questions.explanation: All advertising — including internet ads, social media, business cards, and yard signs — must include the registered name of the brokerage. A sales associate's name alone is never sufficient.

questions.question_text: A sales associate posts a property listing on social media using only their personal name and phone number. This is:
questions.correct_answer: A blind ad violation
questions.explanation: Advertising without the brokerage name is a blind ad, which is prohibited in Florida. All advertising, including social media posts, must clearly include the registered brokerage name.

questions.question_text: A sales team wants to advertise under their team name. What additional requirement must be met?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage name must be included and identifiable
distractors: The team must register with FREC
Team advertising is prohibited in Florida
Each member's license number must appear
questions.explanation: Team names are allowed in Florida advertising, but the brokerage name must also be included and clearly identifiable. The team name cannot replace or obscure the registered brokerage name.

questions.question_text: Which type of advertising is explicitly prohibited under Florida license law?
questions.correct_answer: Blind ads without the brokerage name
distractors: Using the broker's photograph in ads
questions.explanation: Blind ads — any advertising that does not include the registered brokerage name — are prohibited. This applies to all media including print, internet, social media, email, and signage.

questions.question_text: Can a Florida sales associate register their own trade name for real estate business?
questions.correct_answer: No, only brokers may register trade names
questions.explanation: A Florida sales associate may NOT register a trade name. Only brokers and brokerage entities may register and operate under a trade name (DBA) with DBPR.

questions.question_text: A corporation wants to register as a Florida real estate brokerage. What is required regarding licensure?
questions.correct_answer: At least one officer or director must be an active broker
distractors: All officers must be licensed brokers
A majority of directors must be licensed
questions.explanation: For a corporation to register as a brokerage, at least one officer or director must hold an active Florida broker license. The same rule applies to LLCs (one member or manager) and partnerships (one partner).

questions.question_text: A sales associate forms an LLC to receive commission income. This LLC may be used for:
questions.correct_answer: Tax purposes only
questions.explanation: Sales associates may form PAs, LLCs, or corporations for income tax purposes only. The entity does not change their employment relationship with the employing broker or allow independent operation.

questions.question_text: Which business entity type may register as a real estate brokerage in Florida?
questions.correct_answer: Sole proprietorship, partnership, corporation, and LLC
distractors: Corporations and LLCs only, not sole proprietorships
Sole proprietorships only, not other entity types
Partnerships and corporations only, not LLCs
questions.explanation: Florida allows sole proprietorships, partnerships, corporations, and LLCs to register as real estate brokerages. Each must have at least one active licensed broker in a qualifying role.

questions.question_text: An LLC registers as a Florida brokerage. Who within the LLC must be a licensed broker?
questions.correct_answer: At least one member or manager
distractors: No individual — the LLC holds the license
questions.explanation: An LLC brokerage must have at least one member or manager who is an active licensed broker. This person serves as the qualifying broker responsible for the brokerage's operations.

questions.question_text: A sole proprietor wants to operate as a Florida real estate brokerage. What must they hold?
questions.correct_answer: An active broker license
distractors: A sales associate license with 5 years' experience
A partnership agreement with another broker
questions.explanation: A sole proprietor operating as a brokerage must be a registered broker. Unlike other entity types, there is no separation between the individual and the business — the broker IS the brokerage.
